persona_updates.new_value: I am not good enough, I will fail if I make a mistake, I am defined by others' opinions

evidence_citations.excerpt: I keep hearing that comment about my weight in my head, and it's hard to separate what I think of myself from what people say.
I keep replaying that comment about my weight, and it feels like a constant reminder that I'm not good enough.
I keep hearing that comment about my weight in my head, and it's hard to separate what I think of myself from what people say.
Maybe it’s the sense that I’ve lost my discipline, or that people see me differently now.

transcript.text: Moving into this empty space feels like a blank canvas, but also a reminder that I'm just... empty. I keep hearing that comment about my weight in my head, and it's hard to separate what I think of myself from what people say. I feel like I can't produce anything new, and the whole thing just makes me anxious about showing my work.

The empty space of your new apartment is feeling overwhelming, and it's like that blank canvas is staring back at you, making you question your own worth. It's really tough to deal with that comment about your weight echoing in your head, and it's understandable that you're struggling to separate what you think of yourself from what others say. Your anxiety about showing your work is rooted in a deep fear of not being good enough, and it's okay to feel that way. Can you think of a time when you've created something that made you feel proud and good about yourself, like your comic book project?
I don't even remember what made me proud, honestly. The whole thing feels like I'm just... empty, and I keep thinking everyone will see me as a failure.

The weight of that comment is still really affecting you, and it's like it's blocking your ability to remember the things that made you proud in the past. It's understandable that you're feeling empty and worried about what others will think, especially when it comes to your work and this new apartment. It sounds like that fear of being seen as a failure is really holding you back right now. Can you tell me more about what's making it so hard for you to shake off that comment and focus on your own strengths and accomplishments?

I think the comment keeps looping in my head, and every time I look at myself I see a version of me that feels out of sync with the discipline I used to uphold. The apartment's emptiness feels like a mirror of that same emptiness, and I find it hard to focus on my designs because I’m terrified of showing anything that might be judged as a failure. I just feel like I’m not the same person I was before, and that makes me anxious about stepping into any new space—whether it’s a room or a project.

The emptiness of your new apartment is mirroring the emptiness you feel inside, and it's like that comment about your weight has become a constant reminder of your perceived shortcomings. You're feeling out of sync with the discipline you used to uphold, and that's making it hard for you to focus on your designs and step into new projects or spaces. It's like you're seeing a version of yourself that you don't recognize, and that's really scary. Can you think of what small step you could take to start reconnecting with your past discipline and identity?

Honestly, I don't know what a small step would even be; just sketching feels like a pointless exercise, and every line feels like a test of my worth. The apartment and the comment make me feel like I'm failing at even the simplest things.

The emptiness of your new apartment and the weight of that comment are really bearing down on you, making it feel like every little thing is a test of your worth. It's like you're seeing a version of yourself that you don't recognize, and that's really scary. Yeah, it's tough to know where to start when everything feels pointless. Can you think of something, anything, that you used to enjoy doing, even if it feels small or insignificant right now?

I keep replaying that comment about my weight, and it feels like a constant reminder that I'm not good enough. The apartment feels like a void, and every sketch feels like a test of my worth. I can't even think of a small step that would help. Maybe I'm just stuck.

The comment about your weight is still really affecting you, and it's like it's blocking your ability to see yourself in a positive light. You're feeling stuck, and it's like every sketch, every line, is a test of your worth. That can be a really difficult cycle to break. I want to help you explore what's underneath that feeling of being stuck - what is it about that comment that's still resonating with you, and how can we start to shift your focus away from it?

Honestly, I can’t pin down exactly why that comment keeps looping—it feels like every time I look at myself I just see a version that’s out of sync with who I used to be. Maybe it’s the sense that I’ve lost my discipline, or that people see me differently now. I’m not sure how to separate that from my own worth, and the new apartment just adds to the feeling of being a stranger in my own space.

You're feeling really disconnected from the person you used to be, and that comment about your weight is still echoing in your head, making it hard to see yourself in a positive light. It's like you're looking at a version of yourself that you don't recognize, and that's really scary. The emptiness of your new apartment is mirroring the emptiness you feel inside, and it's like every little thing is a test of your worth. Yeah, it's tough to know where to start when everything feels pointless. Can you tell me more about what you think it would take to start feeling like yourself again, like you're reconnecting with that discipline and identity that used to be so important to you?

I keep looping back to that comment, and it feels like it’s defining me. I can’t see how to step out of this self-doubt, and the new space just adds to the feeling of being lost. Maybe I need to find a way to reconnect with my discipline, but I’m not sure what that looks like at all.

That comment about your weight is still really affecting you, and it's like it's become a defining feature of how you see yourself right now. You're feeling lost and unsure of how to step out of this self-doubt, and the new space isn't helping. Yeah, it's tough to know where to start when everything feels like it's tied to that one comment. Can you think of what it would take for you to start feeling like yourself again, like you're reconnecting with that discipline and identity that used to be so important to you? Maybe we can explore what small steps you could take to start rebuilding your sense of self-worth, beyond what others think.
I don't know what to do. Every time I look at my reflection I feel like a stranger, and the apartment just amplifies that. I feel like I can't trust my own judgment anymore.

You're feeling like a stranger in your own skin, and that's a really painful and uncomfortable place to be. It's like every time you look at your reflection, you're seeing a version of yourself that you don't recognize, and that's making it hard for you to trust your own judgment. I can see how the comment about your weight has been echoing in your head, and how the new apartment is amplifying those feelings of disconnection. Can you tell me more about what you think it would take for you to start feeling like yourself again, like you're reconnecting with that discipline and identity that used to be so important to you?

I’m not really sure what that would look like. Every time I think about trying, I feel like I’m stepping into a space where I’m being judged. Maybe I just need something small to remind me of my discipline, but I don’t know what that is.

You're feeling like you're stepping into a space where you're being judged, and that's making it hard for you to connect with your discipline and identity. It's like every little thing is a test of your worth, and that's a really tough cycle to break. One thing that might help is to think about what small actions you used to take that made you feel proud of yourself, like working on your comic book project. Maybe we can explore what it would take for you to start feeling like yourself again, like you're reconnecting with that discipline and identity that used to be so important to you. What do you think is the smallest step you could take to start rebuilding your sense of self-worth?
I guess I could try sketching something, but every line feels like a judgment. Maybe I should just set up a small corner in the apartment and see if that helps, but I'm not sure.

You're feeling like every line you sketch is a judgment, and that's a really tough way to feel. Setting up a small corner in the apartment might be a good place to start, like you mentioned - it could be a small step towards feeling more comfortable and in control. What do you think you'd need to make that space feel like yours, like a reflection of your discipline and identity?
